images.caption: Figure 1:Toy example illustrating head-of-line blocking under FCFS and the benefit of duration-aware scheduling. Three requests arrive in orderR1,R2,R3R_{1},R_{2},R_{3}with audio durations88s,44s, and22s. We assume a constant encoder cost of11s per request and a decoding rate of55output tokens/s, yielding4040,2020, and1010output tokens, respectively. Under FCFS, servingR1R_{1}first delays the two shorter requests and yields an average end-to-end latency of(5+8+10)/3=7.66(5{+}8{+}10)/3=7.66s. Reordering by shortest job first (SJF) reduces the average to(2+5+10)/3=5.66(2{+}5{+}10)/3=5.66s (a1.4×1.4\timesimprovement). All numbers are illustrative and not intended to match measured system timings.

Figure 2:Scatter plots showing the relationship between audio duration and ASR output token count. (a) On the LibriSpeech English test set, token count increases linearly with audio duration, indicating a strong correlation. (b) On the FLEURS test sets for Spanish, Hindi, and Arabic, the linear duration–token relationship is maintained, demonstrating that this correlation generalizes across typologically diverse languages.
Figure 3:LibriSpeech test-clean: End-to-end latency scaling (11–2525req/s). (a) SJF reducesP​50P50E2EL by up to73%73\%at2525req/s, (b) whileP​90P90E2EL reveals the starvation trade-off at high load.

Figure 4:LibriSpeech test-clean: Percentage change in E2EL versus FCFS (11–2525req/s). Negative values indicate improvement. SJF’sP​50P50gains deepen monotonically with load, whileP​90P90degradation emerges beyond1515req/s.

Figure 5:Synthetic split: End-to-end latency scaling (11–3030req/s). (a) SJF’sP​50P50E2EL advantage (−67%-67\%at2525req/s) persists under a uniform duration distribution, (b) whileP​90P90E2EL degradation is moderated compared to LibriSpeech’s right-skewed workload.
Figure 6:Synthetic split: Percentage change in E2EL versus FCFS (11–3030req/s). SJF’s E2EL gains match LibriSpeech, confirming reordering benefits are not an artifact of the natural duration skew.

Figure 7:Request throughput for both workloads. All scheduling policies achieve identical throughput, saturating at∼18\sim 18req/s on LibriSpeech and∼13\sim 13req/s on the synthetic split, confirming negligible scheduling overhead.
Figure 8:LibriSpeech TTFT scaling (1–25 req/s). SJF keepsP​50P50TTFT below300300ms at all rates while FCFS exceeds44s.
Figure 9:LibriSpeech: percentage change in TTFT versus FCFS (11–2525req/s). SJF’sP​50P50TTFT gains deepen monotonically, reaching−93%-93\%at2525req/s.
Figure 10:Synthetic split TTFT scaling (11-3030req/s). SJF achieves−84%-84\%P​50P50TTFT at2525req/s, confirming that reordering benefits are independent of duration variance.
Figure 11:Synthetic split: percentage change in TTFT versus FCFS (11-3030req/s). The pattern closely mirrors LibriSpeech, confirming robustness to duration distribution.

Figure 12:Burst workload results (rate=∞=\infty,500500simultaneous requests). Bar heights show absolute metric values; annotations indicate percentage change versus FCFS. HRRN provides the most consistent improvements across all latency metrics.

Figure 13:Whisper-Medium E2EL scaling (1515–2525req/s).P​25P25latency (a) shows that SJF benefits the bottom quartile of requests across all rates, whileP​90P90latency (b) reveals the tail cost at high load, consistent with Large-v3 findings.
